title: SEGMENT 9 — The resurgence: antibiotic resistance and the return of phage

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — The resurgence: antibiotic resistance and the return of phage
The hinge of the modern phage therapy story is antibiotic resistance.
We covered the resistance problem at length in the antibiotics lecture, so I'll just summarize the relevant piece.
Over the seventy years since penicillin came into widespread use, bacteria have evolved resistance to essentially every antibiotic we've ever deployed.
The pace of resistance evolution has outstripped the pace of new antibiotic discovery by a wide margin.
The pipeline of new antibacterial drugs has been thin for decades.

Some bacteria — notably the so-called ESKAPE pathogens: Enterococcus faecium, Staphylococcus aureus, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Acinetobacter baumannii, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Enterobacter — have evolved strains that are resistant to essentially every clinically available antibiotic, including the last-resort drugs.
Pan-resistant is the term of art.
There are patients dying in hospitals today because there is no antibiotic left to give them.
In that context, doctors and biologists started, in the late 1990s and accelerating through the 2010s, to look back at phage therapy with fresh eyes.
Suddenly the Soviet tradition looked less like backward folk medicine and more like an unjustly ignored alternative.
Suddenly the precision of phages — their species-specific killing, evolution-aware narrow spectrum — looked less like a limitation and more like an advantage.

Suddenly the fact that phages co-evolve with their bacterial targets, that they can in principle keep up with resistance as it emerges, looked like an evolutionary feature rather than an unpredictable bug.
The phage therapy resurgence, in short, was driven by desperation — and the desperation forced a reconsideration of what the precision of phages actually meant.
Companies started forming.

Intralytix, founded in 1998 in Baltimore, became one of the first Western phage therapy companies and got FDA approval in 2006 for an anti-Listeria phage cocktail used in food safety — we'll come back to that.

Locust Biosciences, Adaptive Phage Therapeutics, Armata Pharmaceuticals, Pherecydes Pharma in France, BiomX in Israel, Felix Biotechnology — by the late 2010s and early 2020s, a small but growing biotech industry was building phage products, mostly for compassionate-use cases initially and increasingly toward formal regulatory approval.
The Eliava Institute became a partner and a consultant to many of these companies, providing phage libraries, methodologies, and clinical experience accumulated over decades.
The clinical successes that captured the public imagination came in the late 2010s.
The most famous, and the one that genuinely shifted the field, was the Tom Patterson case at UCSD in 2016 and 2017.
Patterson — a psychiatry researcher at UCSD — contracted a multi-drug-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii infection during a vacation in Egypt.
He was airlifted home, slowly deteriorated despite every available antibiotic, and was in a coma in a hospital bed in San Diego, dying.
His wife, Steffanie Strathdee — an epidemiologist at UCSD — refused to accept the prognosis and started researching alternatives.
She found the phage therapy tradition.
She started cold-calling phage researchers around the world, asking if anyone had phages against A. baumannii.
A network mobilized — phage labs at Texas A&M, the U.
S.

Navy Medical Research Center, and other institutions screened their libraries, found phages active against Patterson's specific bacterial isolate, and on an emergency compassionate-use basis, the FDA approved infusing those phages into Patterson intravenously.
Within days of starting the phage treatment, Patterson woke up.
Within weeks, the Acinetobacter infection cleared.
He survived.
Pause.
The Patterson case became, justifiably, a media phenomenon.
Strathdee wrote a book about it — The Perfect Predator — and the case became the rallying example for the resurgence.
UCSD founded the Center for Innovative Phage Applications and Therapeutics, IPATH, in 2018 — the first dedicated phage therapy center at a major American academic medical center.
Compassionate-use cases started accumulating.

The 2019 case of Spencer, the cystic fibrosis patient at Great Ormond Street in London who received engineered phages for a Mycobacterium abscessus infection — that case used phages from Graham Hatfull's lab at Pittsburgh, and the engineering involved knocking out a lysogeny gene to convert a lysogenic phage into an obligately lytic one.
The CF patient survived.

Other compassionate-use cases — for endocarditis, prosthetic joint infections, deep wound infections — accumulated, each one published as a case report, each one adding to the slowly-rebuilding clinical evidence base.
By 2026, where we sit now, formal phase 2 and phase 3 clinical trials are underway for several phage products.

The FDA has not yet approved a phage therapy for human systemic infection — that's still pending — but compassionate-use access has been streamlined, and the regulatory pathway is starting to crystallize.
Phage therapy is no longer a fringe alternative.
It's an active, growing area of pharmaceutical development, with billions of dollars of investment, dozens of companies, and a small but real clinical pipeline.
The hibernation is over.
